content:
# Genesis Chapter 19 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 10v-11r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_019_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_019_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 14-15 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0018

---

*The Two Angels Arrive at Sodom — Lot's Hospitality (vv. 1–3)*

**19:1** And the two angels [וַיָּבֹאוּ שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים] came to Sodom [סְדֹמָה] in the evening [בָּעֶרֶב] and Lot was sitting [וְלוֹט יֹשֵׁב] at the gate of Sodom [בְּשַׁעַר-סְדֹם] and Lot saw [וַיַּרְא-לוֹט] and he rose to meet them [וַיָּקָם לִקְרָאתָם] and he bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] with his face to the ground [אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה].

**19:2** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Look now [הִנֵּה נָּא] — my lords [אֲדֹנַי] — turn aside please [סוּרוּ נָא] to your servant's house [אֶל-בֵּית עַבְדְּכֶם] and spend the night [וְלִינוּ] and wash your feet [וְרַחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיכֶם] and you shall rise early [וַהֲשְׁכַּמְתֶּם] and go on your way [וַהֲלַכְתֶּם לְדַרְכְּכֶם] — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: No [לֹא] — for in the open square [כִּי בָרְחוֹב] we will spend the night [נָלִין].

**19:3** And he pressed them greatly [וַיִּפְצַר-בָּם מְאֹד] and they turned aside to him [וַיָּסֻרוּ אֵלָיו] and they came to his house [וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ] and he made for them a feast [וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם מִשְׁתֶּה] and he baked unleavened bread [וּמַצּוֹת אָפָה] and they ate [וַיֹּאכֵלוּ].

---

*The Men of Sodom Surround the House — The Demand (vv. 4–11)*

**19:4** Before they lay down [טֶרֶם יִשְׁכָּבוּ] — the men of the city [וְאַנְשֵׁי הָעִיר] — the men of Sodom [אַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם] — surrounded the house [נָסַבּוּ עַל-הַבַּיִת] — from young [מִנַּעַר] to old [וְעַד-זָקֵן] — all the people from the farthest end [כָּל-הָעָם מִקָּצֶה].

**19:5** And they called to Lot [וַיִּקְרְאוּ אֶל-לוֹט] and they said to him [וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ]: Where are the men [אַיֵּה הָאֲנָשִׁים] who came to you tonight [אֲשֶׁר-בָּאוּ אֵלֶיךָ הַלַּיְלָה] — bring them out [הוֹצִיאֵם] to us [אֵלֵינוּ] that we may know them [וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם].

**19:6** And Lot went out [וַיֵּצֵא אֲלֵהֶם לוֹט] to them at the entrance [הַפֶּתְחָה] and the door he shut behind him [וְהַדֶּלֶת סָגַר אַחֲרָיו].

**19:7** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Please, my brothers [אַל-נָא אַחַי] — do not act wickedly [תָּרֵעוּ].

**19:8** Look now [הִנֵּה-נָּא] — I have two daughters [לִי שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת] who have not known [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדְעוּ] a man [אִישׁ] — let me now bring them out to you [אוֹצִיאָה-נָּא אֶתְהֶן אֲלֵיכֶם] and do to them [וַעֲשׂוּ לָהֶן] as is good in your eyes [כַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶם] — only to these men do nothing [רַק לָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵל אַל-תַּעֲשׂוּ דָבָר] — for on this account they came [כִּי-עַל-כֵּן בָּאוּ] under the shadow of my roof-beam [בְּצֵל קֹרָתִי].

**19:9** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Stand back [גֶּשׁ-הָלְאָה] — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: The one came as a sojourner [הָאֶחָד בָּא-לָגוּר] and he has been judging and judging [וְשָׁפוֹט יִשְׁפֹּט] — now we will deal more wickedly with you [עַתָּה נָרַע לְךָ מֵהֶם] than with them — and they pressed against the man [וַיִּפְצְרוּ בָאִישׁ בְּלוֹט] — against Lot — greatly [מְאֹד] and they drew near [וַיִּגְּשׁוּ] to break open the door [לִשְׁבֹּר הַדָּלֶת].

**19:10** And the men reached out their hand [וַיִּשְׁלְחוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אֶת-יָדָם] and brought Lot in [וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת-לוֹט] to them into the house [אֲלֵיהֶם הַבָּיְתָה] and the door they shut [וְאֶת-הַדֶּלֶת סָגָרוּ].

**19:11** And the men who were at the entrance of the house [וְאֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר-פֶּתַח הַבַּיִת] they struck with blindness [הִכּוּ בַּסַּנְוֵרִים] — from small to great [מִקָּטֹן וְעַד-גָּדוֹל] — and they wearied themselves [וַיִּלְאוּ] to find the entrance [לִמְצֹא הַפָּתַח].

---

*Lot Warned to Flee — The Offer of Escape (vv. 12–22)*

**19:12** And the men said to Lot [וַיֹּאמְרוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אֶל-לוֹט]: Who else do you have here [עֹד מִי-לְךָ פֹה] — son-in-law [חָתָן] and your sons [וּבָנֶיךָ] and your daughters [וּבְנֹתֶיךָ] and all who are yours in the city [וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-לְךָ בָּעִיר] — bring out [הוֹצֵא] from the place [מִן-הַמָּקוֹם].

**19:13** For we are going to destroy [כִּי-מַשְׁחִתִים אֲנַחְנוּ] this place [אֶת-הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה] — because the outcry of them [כִּי-גָדְלָה צַעֲקָתָם] has grown great before the face of the LORD [אֶת-פְּנֵי יְהוָה] and the LORD has sent us [וַיְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ יְהוָה] to destroy it [לְשַׁחֲתָהּ].

**19:14** And Lot went out [וַיֵּצֵא לוֹט] and he spoke to his sons-in-law [וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-חֲתָנָיו] — those who had taken his daughters [לֹקְחֵי בְנֹתָיו] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Arise [קוּמוּ] — go out [צְאוּ] from this place [מִן-הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה] — for the LORD is going to destroy [כִּי-מַשְׁחִית יְהוָה] the city [אֶת-הָעִיר] — and he was as one who jests [וַיְהִי כִמְצַחֵק] in the eyes of his sons-in-law [בְּעֵינֵי חֲתָנָיו].

**19:15** And as the dawn came up [וּכְמוֹ הַשַּׁחַר עָלָה] the angels urged Lot [וַיָּאִיצוּ הַמַּלְאָכִים בְּלוֹט] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Arise [קוּם] — take [קַח] your wife [אֶת-אִשְׁתְּךָ] and your two daughters who are found [וְאֶת-שְׁתֵּי בְנֹתֶיךָ הַנִּמְצָאֹת] — lest you be swept away [פֶּן-תִּסָּפֶה] in the iniquity of the city [בַּעֲוֹן הָעִיר].

**19:16** And he lingered [וַיִּתְמַהְמָהּ] and the men took hold [וַיַּחֲזִיקוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] of his hand [בְּיָדוֹ] and of the hand of his wife [וּבְיַד-אִשְׁתּוֹ] and of the hand of his two daughters [וּבְיַד שְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו] — in the compassion of the LORD upon him [בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו] — and they brought him out [וַיֹּצִאֻהוּ] and they set him outside the city [וַיַּנִּחֻהוּ מִחוּץ לָעִיר].

**19:17** And it came to pass when they brought them out outside [וַיְהִי כְהוֹצִיאָם אֹתָם הַחוּצָה] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Flee for your life [הִמָּלֵט עַל-נַפְשֶׁךָ] — do not look behind you [אַל-תַּבִּיט אַחֲרֶיךָ] and do not stand [וְאַל-תַּעֲמֹד] in all the plain [בְּכָל-הַכִּכָּר] — to the mountain flee [הָהָרָה הִמָּלֵט] lest you be swept away [פֶּן-תִּסָּפֶה].

**19:18** And Lot said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹט אֲלֵהֶם]: Please — no [אַל-נָא] — my lord [אֲדֹנָי].

**19:19** Look now [הִנֵּה-נָּא] — your servant has found favor [מָצָא עַבְדְּךָ חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ] and you have made great your kindness [וַתַּגְדֵּל חַסְדְּךָ] which you did with me [אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי] to keep my life alive [לְהַחֲיוֹת אֶת-נַפְשִׁי] — but I am not able [וְאָנֹכִי לֹא אוּכַל] to flee to the mountain [לְהִמָּלֵט הָהָרָה] lest the evil overtake me [פֶּן-תִּדְבָּקַנִי הָרָעָה] and I die [וָמַתִּי].

**19:20** Look now [הִנֵּה-נָּא] — this city is near [הָעִיר הַזֹּאת קְרֹבָה] to flee there [לָנוּס שָׁמָּה] and it is a small thing [וְהִוא מִצְעָר] — please let me flee there [אִמָּלְטָה נָּא שָׁמָּה] — is it not a small thing [הֲלֹא מִצְעָר הִוא] — that my life may live [וּתְחִי נַפְשִׁי].

**19:21** And he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: Look — I have lifted your face [הִנֵּה נָשָׂאתִי פָנֶיךָ] also in this thing [גַּם לַדָּבָר הַזֶּה] — not to overthrow [לְבִלְתִּי הָפְכִּי] the city [אֶת-הָעִיר] of which you have spoken [אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ].

**19:22** Make haste [מַהֵר] — flee there [הִמָּלֵט שָׁמָּה] — for I am not able [כִּי לֹא אוּכַל] to do anything [לַעֲשׂוֹת דָּבָר] until you arrive there [עַד-בֹּאֲךָ שָׁמָּה] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] the name of the city was called Zoar [קָרָא שֵׁם-הָעִיר צוֹעַר].

---

*The Destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (vv. 23–25)*

**19:23** The sun had risen [הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ יָצָא] over the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ] when Lot came to Zoar [וְלוֹט בָּא צֹעֲרָה].

**19:24** And the LORD rained down [וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר] upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah [עַל-סְדֹם וְעַל-עֲמֹרָה] sulfur and fire [גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ] — from the LORD [מֵאֵת יְהוָה] — from the heavens [מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם].

**19:25** And he overturned [וַיַּהֲפֹךְ] those cities [אֶת-הֶעָרִים הָאֵל] and all the plain [וְאֵת כָּל-הַכִּכָּר] and all the inhabitants of the cities [וְאֵת כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי הֶעָרִים] and what grew [וְצֶמַח] on the ground [הָאֲדָמָה].

---

*Lot's Wife — A Pillar of Salt (v. 26)*

**19:26** And his wife looked back [וַתַּבֵּט אִשְׁתּוֹ מֵאַחֲרָיו] from behind him [מֵאַחֲרָיו] and she became a pillar of salt [וַתְּהִי נְצִיב מֶלַח].

---

*Abraham Sees the Destruction (vv. 27–28)*

**19:27** And Abraham rose early in the morning [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר] to the place [אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם] where he had stood [אֲשֶׁר-עָמַד שָׁם] before the LORD [אֶת-פְּנֵי יְהוָה].

**19:28** And he looked down [וַיַּשְׁקֵף] over the face of Sodom and Gomorrah [עַל-פְּנֵי סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה] and over all the face of the land of the plain [וְעַל-כָּל-פְּנֵי אֶרֶץ הַכִּכָּר] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] and look [וְהִנֵּה] — the smoke of the land went up [עָלָה קִיטֹר הָאָרֶץ] like the smoke of a furnace [כְּקִיטֹר הַכִּבְשָׁן].

---

*Lot Saved for Abraham's Sake (v. 29)*

**19:29** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when God destroyed [בְּשַׁחֵת אֱלֹהִים] the cities of the plain [אֶת-עָרֵי הַכִּכָּר] that God remembered Abraham [וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] and he sent Lot out [וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת-לוֹט] from the midst of the overturning [מִתּוֹךְ הַהֲפֵכָה] when he overturned [בַּהֲפֹךְ] the cities [אֶת-הֶעָרִים] among which [אֲשֶׁר-יָשַׁב] Lot had dwelt.

---

*Lot and His Daughters in the Cave (vv. 30–38)*

**19:30** And Lot went up [וַיַּעַל לוֹט] from Zoar [מִצּוֹעַר] and he dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב] in the mountain [בָּהָר] and his two daughters with him [וּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו עִמּוֹ] — for he was afraid [כִּי יָרֵא] to dwell in Zoar [לָשֶׁבֶת בְּצוֹעַר] — and he dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב] in a cave [בַּמְּעָרָה] — he and his two daughters [הוּא וּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתָיו].

**19:31** And the firstborn said [וַתֹּאמֶר הַבְּכִירָה] to the younger [אֶל-הַצְּעִירָה]: Our father is old [אָבִינוּ זָקֵן] and there is no man [וְאִישׁ אֵין] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ] to come in to us [לָבוֹא עָלֵינוּ] according to the way of all the earth [כְּדֶרֶךְ כָּל-הָאָרֶץ].

**19:32** Come [לְכָה] — let us make our father drink wine [נַשְׁקֶה אֶת-אָבִינוּ יַיִן] and let us lie with him [וְנִשְׁכְּבָה עִמּוֹ] that we may preserve seed from our father [וּנְחַיֶּה מֵאָבִינוּ זָרַע].

**19:33** And they made their father drink wine [וַתַּשְׁקֶיןָ אֶת-אֲבִיהֶן יַיִן] on that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא] and the firstborn went in [וַתָּבֹא הַבְּכִירָה] and lay with her father [וַתִּשְׁכַּב אֶת-אָבִיהָ] and he did not know [וְלֹא-יָדַע] when she lay down [בְּשִׁכְבָהּ] and when she arose [וּבְקוּמָהּ].

**19:34** And it came to pass on the morrow [וַיְהִי מִמָּחֳרָת] and the firstborn said [וַתֹּאמֶר הַבְּכִירָה] to the younger [אֶל-הַצְּעִירָה]: Look [הֵן] — I lay last night [שָׁכַבְתִּי אֶמֶשׁ] with my father [אֶת-אָבִי] — let us make him drink wine also tonight [נַשְׁקֶנּוּ יַיִן גַּם-הַלַּיְלָה] and go in [וּבֹאִי] — lie with him [שִׁכְבִי עִמּוֹ] that we may preserve seed from our father [וּנְחַיֶּה מֵאָבִינוּ זָרַע].

**19:35** And they made their father drink wine also on that night [וַתַּשְׁקֶיןָ גַּם בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא אֶת-אֲבִיהֶן יַיִן] and the younger arose [וַתָּקָם הַצְּעִירָה] and lay with him [וַתִּשְׁכַּב עִמּוֹ] and he did not know [וְלֹא-יָדַע] when she lay down [בְּשִׁכְבָהּ] and when she arose [וּבְקוּמָהּ].

**19:36** And the two daughters of Lot conceived [וַתַּהֲרֶיןָ שְׁתֵּי בְנוֹת-לוֹט] from their father [מֵאֲבִיהֶן].

**19:37** And the firstborn bore a son [וַתֵּלֶד הַבְּכִירָה בֵּן] and she called his name Moab [וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ מוֹאָב] — he is the father of Moab [הוּא אֲבִי-מוֹאָב] to this day [עַד-הַיּוֹם].

**19:38** And the younger — she also bore a son [וְהַצְּעִירָה גַם-הִוא יָלְדָה בֵּן] and she called his name Ben-Ammi [וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ בֶּן-עַמִּי] — he is the father of the sons of Ammon [הוּא אֲבִי בְנֵי-עַמּוֹן] to this day [עַד-הַיּוֹם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 19:1 — שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים: The Two Angels Arrive (Key Crux — Continuity with Genesis 18)

**שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים** — "the two angels." This is the first explicit identification in the narrative. In Genesis 18, three men arrived at Mamre; they are never called angels in that chapter. Here, as two of the three arrive at Sodom, the narrator calls them **הַמַּלְאָכִים** (hammal'akhim) — "the angels / the messengers." The use of the definite article **הַ-** ("the two angels") signals that these are figures the reader already knows — the same two who left Mamre in 18:22. The third, who remained to converse with Abraham through the intercession of 18:23-32, is now identified implicitly as YHWH himself (as confirmed by the text of ch. 18). The narrative thus quietly resolves the identity question that ch. 18 left open: YHWH is not among the two who arrive at Sodom; the two who come are the angelic messengers.

**לוֹט יֹשֵׁב בְּשַׁעַר-סְדֹם** — "Lot was sitting at the gate of Sodom." Sitting at the city gate is the posture of civic authority and participation in public life — the gate is where legal matters are adjudicated, where elders sit in judgment, where commerce is transacted (Ruth 4:1-12; Prov. 31:23). Lot's sitting at the gate indicates that he has not remained an outsider in Sodom but has become integrated into its civic life. This detail sets up the irony and moral complexity of his position: he is a man of the city, yet he will act with the hospitality code of the desert sojourner. He recognizes the angels and responds exactly as Abraham did in 18:2 — rising, running, bowing. The hospitality reflex is still in him.

**וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה** — "and he bowed down with his face to the ground." The identical prostration gesture as Abraham's in 18:2 — the literary echo is deliberate. Lot in Sodom mirrors Abraham at Mamre: both receive divine visitors; both offer hospitality; both are defended by angels; but only Abraham negotiates with YHWH in the open, while Lot negotiates in the shadows of a doomed city.

---

### On 19:2 — לִינוּ בָרְחוֹב: The Angels' Refusal (Hospitality Protocol)

**לֹא כִּי בָרְחוֹב נָלִין** — "No — for in the open square we will spend the night."

The angels initially decline the invitation. This detail is significant: the same angels immediately accepted Abraham's invitation in 18:5 ("So shall you do as you have spoken"). The difference reveals the narrative purpose: at Mamre, the angels accepted hospitality in a safe environment; at Sodom, their initial refusal and then capitulation to Lot's pressing urgency indicates that the invitation is necessary for their safety — and more pointedly, that in Sodom, for strangers to sleep in the open square is to be at the mercy of the city's violence. Lot's insistence thus already contains within it his knowledge of Sodom's character. He knows what the square at night means.

**וַיִּפְצַר-בָּם מְאֹד** — "and he pressed them greatly." The verb **פָּצַר** (patsar) means "to press, to urge with insistence." The same verb reappears in v. 9 where the men of Sodom press against Lot. The contrast between Lot's urging hospitality (v. 3) and the mob's urging violence (v. 9) is the chapter's central structural tension: welcome vs. assault, protection vs. violation.

---

### On 19:3 — מַצּוֹת: Unleavened Bread (A Textual Detail)

**וּמַצּוֹת אָפָה** — "and he baked unleavened bread." Unlike Abraham who made cakes from fine flour (**עֻגוֹת** from **קֶמַח סֹלֶת**, 18:6), Lot specifically bakes **מַצּוֹת** — unleavened bread. The rabbis noted this detail: the use of **מַצּוֹת** rather than leavened bread has been connected variously to haste (no time to let bread rise — as in the Exodus, Exod. 12:39), to Passover typology, or simply to Lot's more modest household provision. The detail does not require allegorical reading; it is a realistic narrative touch. But it has generated extensive commentary, and the EMET notes it so the reader is aware.

---

### On 19:5 — וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם: yada' — "That We May Know Them" (Key Crux)

**הוֹצִיאֵם אֵלֵינוּ וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם** — "bring them out to us that we may know them."

This is one of the most contested phrases in Genesis — the crux on which enormous historical, theological, and ethical interpretation has turned.

**The verb יָדַע (yada') — the range of meaning:**

The root **יָדַע** has a wide semantic range in Biblical Hebrew:
1. **Cognitive knowing:** "I know this fact" — ordinary information
2. **Relational/experiential knowing:** "to know personally, to be acquainted with" — which can shade into intimacy
3. **Sexual knowing / euphemism for intercourse:** Genesis 4:1 ("Adam knew his wife and she conceived"), 4:17, 4:25, 19:8 (Lot says his daughters "have not known a man"), 24:16 (Rebekah "had not known a man"). This is a well-established Biblical Hebrew idiom.

**The exegetical question:** Does **וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם** in 19:5 mean:
- **(A) "that we may know them" in the sexual sense** — i.e., a demand for gang rape of the male visitors — the interpretation virtually universal in the history of Christian exegesis (patristic, medieval, Reformation) and much of modern scholarship?
- **(B) "that we may become acquainted with them"** — i.e., the men of Sodom want to inspect or interrogate the strangers, perhaps enforcing a rule against unauthorized visitors, perhaps hostile but not necessarily sexually? (This minority view was advanced especially in the mid-20th century by scholars seeking to redirect attention from sexual sin to inhospitality as the primary sin of Sodom.)

**The contextual evidence strongly supports (A):**

Several textual signals favor the sexual reading:
- **Lot's response in v. 8** is decisive: he offers his daughters who "have not known a man" — using the identical **yada'** root — as substitutes. If the crowd's demand were merely for social inspection or interrogation, the offer of his virgin daughters makes no sense as a substitute. The offer is morally horrifying precisely because Lot understands **yada'** in the sexual sense and substitutes one object of sexual violence for another.
- **The parallel in Judges 19:22-25:** The Gibeah narrative is the closest parallel — a Levite's concubine is violently gang-raped after the men of the city demand to "know" the Levite. The parallel structure in Judges (mob surrounds house, demands the visitor for **yada'**, host offers female family members, sexual assault of the substitute occurs) makes the sexual meaning of **yada'** in both Genesis 19 and Judges 19 nearly certain. The two narratives are literarily linked and mutually interpreting.
- **The text's moral horror** throughout the passage is directed at the totality of Sodom's wickedness, of which this demand is the apex. A demand for mere social interrogation would be hostile but not catastrophic; a demand for mass sexual assault of guests is the consummation of the city's violence against the vulnerable and the stranger.

**The "inhospitality only" reading (B) — assessment:**

The argument that **yada'** means only "get acquainted with" and that the sin of Sodom is inhospitality rather than sexual violence has been influential in 20th-century revisionist scholarship (Sherwin Bailey, D.S. Bailey, John J. McNeill). However, the argument faces decisive difficulties: Lot's offer of his daughters (v. 8) cannot be explained without the sexual meaning; the Judges 19 parallel confirms the idiom; and the MT's narrative logic requires the sexual sense for the narrative to cohere. The "inhospitality" reading is not linguistically impossible but is contextually untenable.

**What the text does and does not say about the nature of Sodom's sin:**

It is important to note that Ezekiel 16:49-50 lists Sodom's sins as: **pride, excess of food, and prosperous ease, and she did not strengthen the hand of the poor and needy. They were haughty and committed abomination (תּוֹעֵבָה)** before me. The sin of Sodom in Ezekiel is primarily social injustice — pride, excess, failure to care for the poor — alongside the **tow'evah** (abomination). The Genesis 19 narrative depicts the apex of Sodom's wickedness in the demand of v. 5, which the EMET renders with the literal "that we may know them" — preserving the Hebrew's deliberate and loaded use of **yada'** — and the notes make the full meaning clear.

**The EMET translates:** "that we may know them" — not "that we may have sex with them" (which would add beyond the Hebrew) and not "that we may get acquainted with them" (which would suppress the Hebrew's evident sexual connotation in context). The bracketed Hebrew helps: **וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם**. The reader who follows the notes will understand the full weight of the demand.

---

### On 19:8 — Lot's Offer of His Daughters (Moral Crux)

**הִנֵּה-נָּא לִי שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדְעוּ אִישׁ** — "Look now — I have two daughters who have not known a man."

This is one of the most morally disturbing moments in the entire Hebrew Bible. Lot offers his two virgin daughters to the mob for whatever they wish to do to them — in order to protect his male guests from the same violence. The EMET translates it without softening: **וַעֲשׂוּ לָהֶן כַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֵיכֶם** — "do to them as is good in your eyes."

**Why does Lot do this? The exegetical options:**

1. **Ancient hospitality ethics:** In the ancient Near East, the obligation to protect a guest who had come under one's roof (**בְּצֵל קֹרָתִי** — "under the shadow of my roof-beam") was an absolute social and moral code. A host's honor — and his social standing — depended on the inviolability of the hospitality he had extended. From within this value system, Lot is choosing the lesser violation (the dishonoring of his daughters) to fulfill the greater obligation (the protection of his guests). This does not justify the act; it contextualizes it within a moral framework foreign to modern ethics.

2. **The daughters are property:** In the ancient world, unmarried daughters were under the legal control of the father. The text presents this without comment — and without endorsement. The narrator is not endorsing Lot's action; the narrative consequence (his daughters later sleep with him) can be read as a structural irony: the daughters whom he offered to the mob to be used will later use him.

3. **Desperation and moral collapse:** Lot is pressed on every side, overwhelmed, and his offer is the act of a man in a desperate, impossible situation making a terrible calculation. The fact that the men of Sodom reject even this offer (v. 9) — they want the angels, not the daughters — shows how bottomless the city's wickedness is.

**The phrase בְּצֵל קֹרָתִי** — "under the shadow of my roof-beam" — is unique in Biblical Hebrew. **קֹרָה** (qorah) is a roof-beam, a main structural timber. "The shadow of the roof-beam" extends as far as the house itself — it is an idiom for the protection of the household, the shelter of a host. The same metaphorical field appears in the Psalms' language of God's protective shadow (**בְּצֵל כְּנָפֶיךָ** — "under the shadow of your wings"). Lot invokes the full weight of the hospitality-protection code.

**The text does not moralize here.** It presents Lot's act and the crowd's response without editorial comment. The reader is left to wrestle with the moral horror unaided by the narrator. This is characteristic of the Hebrew narrative style: the text trusts its readers to feel the shock without being told to feel it.

---

### On 19:9 — הָאֶחָד בָּא-לָגוּר: The Accusation Against Lot (Social Crux)

**הָאֶחָד בָּא-לָגוּר וַיִּשְׁפֹּט שָׁפוֹט** — "The one came as a sojourner and he has been judging and judging."

The crowd turns on Lot with a second accusation beyond the demand for his guests: he is a **גֵּר** (ger — sojourner, resident alien) — an outsider — yet he "judges" them. The verb **שָׁפַט** (shafat) in the infinitive absolute construction **שָׁפוֹט יִשְׁפֹּט** intensifies the accusation: "he judges and judges / he keeps on judging." Lot's offer of his daughters (v. 8) and his address "my brothers, do not act wickedly" (v. 7) are being read by the crowd as an assertion of moral authority over them — the sojourner presumes to tell the citizens what is wicked. The crowd resents not only the refusal but the moral stance behind it.

This detail reveals Sodom's deeper pathology: it is not merely sexually violent but morally inverted — a city where moral correction by an outsider is itself treated as an offense. The refusal to hear moral reproof is a signature of the narrative's theology: Sodom is beyond correction, beyond the possibility of the ten righteous whom Abraham sought (18:32). The crowd's response to Lot's moral appeal is to threaten him with worse than they threaten his guests.

---

### On 19:11 — סַנְוֵרִים: Blindness (A Rare Word)

**וַיַּכּוּ אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים... בַּסַּנְוֵרִים** — "they struck the men... with blindness."

**סַנְוֵרִים** (sanverim) is a rare word in Biblical Hebrew — appearing only here and in 2 Kings 6:18 (where Elisha prays for the Syrian army to be struck with the same affliction). It is generally understood as a dazzling blindness or visual disorientation — perhaps a supernatural blinding light — rather than ordinary physical blindness. The Aramaic cognate and Greek **ἀορασία** (LXX: aorasia — "invisibility, lack of sight") support the sense of sudden inability to see. The effect is narrative: the mob at the door is rendered harmless, unable to find the entrance, while Lot remains free to receive the angels' warning.

---

### On 19:13 — מַשְׁחִיתִים אֲנַחְנוּ: "We Are Going to Destroy" (The Angels as Agents)

**כִּי-מַשְׁחִתִים אֲנַחְנוּ אֶת-הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה** — "For we are going to destroy this place."

The angels announce themselves as the agents of destruction — **מַשְׁחִיתִים** (mashchitim), from **שָׁחַת** (shachat), the verb for destruction and corruption (used of the earth in Gen. 6:11-12 before the flood). The sentence explicitly names YHWH as the sender: **וַיְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ יְהוָה לְשַׁחֲתָהּ** — "the LORD has sent us to destroy it." The delegation of destructive agency to angelic messengers while YHWH remains the authorizing source is consistent with the theological pattern of the Hebrew Bible: YHWH sends, the angel executes. The agent of destruction is always in service of a divine judicial decision — the **זַעֲקָה** (outcry) of 18:20 that has reached YHWH has been investigated and confirmed (18:21) and the sentence is now being carried out.

---

### On 19:14 — כִּמְצַחֵק: "As One Who Jests" (Tragic Irony)

**וַיְהִי כִמְצַחֵק בְּעֵינֵי חֲתָנָיו** — "and he was as one who jests in the eyes of his sons-in-law."

**מְצַחֵק** (metsacheq) — from the root **צָחַק** (tsachaq) — the same root as "laughter" and **יִצְחָק** (Isaac). The word means "one who laughs, jests, plays, mocks." Lot's warning is taken as a joke. The irony is profound: within the narrative of Genesis, **צָחַק** has recently been associated with the laughter of Abraham (17:17) and Sarah (18:12-13) at the impossible promise — the laughter that names Isaac. Now the same root names the disbelieving laughter of Lot's sons-in-law at the impossible warning. But where Abraham's and Sarah's laughter was the laughter of astonishment before divine promise, the sons-in-law's laughter is the laughter of fatal disbelief before divine judgment. They will not survive to know which was a jest.

---

### On 19:16 — בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו: Divine Compassion Despite Hesitation (Key Crux)

**וַיִּתְמַהְמָהּ... בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו** — "And he lingered... in the compassion of the LORD upon him."

**וַיִּתְמַהְמָהּ** (wayyitmahmahe) — a reflexive-iterative form expressing prolonged hesitation, delay, lingering. Lot waits. He does not move. The angels have given the warning, but Lot tarries. The reason is not given; the reader can imagine — the house, the possessions, the life he has built in Sodom. And yet the LORD acts despite the hesitation: **בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו** — "in / because of the compassion of the LORD upon him." The Hebrew **חֶמְלָה** (chemlah) is "compassion, tender mercy" — the emotional register of protective, pitying care. YHWH's compassion for Lot overrides Lot's own paralysis; the angels physically seize him and his family and carry them out.

This is one of the most grace-laden images in Genesis: not earned rescue, not deserved escape — Lot hesitates, and God's compassion does not hesitate. The rescue is not because Lot is righteous enough or decisive enough; it is because the LORD is compassionate. This verse stands as a theological counterpoint to the judgment that surrounds it: in the midst of the destruction of cities, divine mercy extracts the one who lingers.

The note is directly connected to 19:29: "God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out." Lot's rescue is explicitly attributed to Abraham's intercessory standing before God. The compassion in v. 16 and the remembering in v. 29 together form a theological bracket around the destruction narrative.

---

### On 19:17 — אַל-תַּבִּיט אַחֲרֶיךָ: "Do Not Look Behind You" (The Command and Its Violation)

**אַל-תַּבִּיט אַחֲרֶיךָ** — "do not look behind you."

The prohibition against looking back is a command of total forward commitment — do not turn, do not dwell on what is being left behind, do not let your body orient toward the destruction. The Hebrew **נָבַט** (navat) in the Hiphil (**תַּבִּיט**) means "to look, to gaze, to contemplate" — it is not the incidental glance of **רָאָה** (ra'ah, "to see") but the deliberate act of turning and looking. The prohibition implies that looking back is itself a form of attachment to what is being destroyed — a turning of the soul as well as the body toward Sodom.

Lot's wife's violation of this command in v. 26 is therefore not merely a physical misstep but a symbol of the orientation of the heart. The judgment that befalls her — transformation into a pillar of salt — is presented without commentary, as stark as the prohibition itself. She looked back and she became what she looked at: frozen, immovable, left behind.

**Jesus's allusion (Luke 17:32):** In the eschatological discourse of Luke 17:28-33, Jesus cites "likewise remember Lot's wife" in the context of warning his disciples against returning to what they have left behind when judgment comes. The image of Lot's wife has become proverbial for the fatal nostalgia of looking back toward what is already under judgment.

---

### On 19:20 — צוֹעַר / מִצְעָר: Zoar — "Small" (Wordplay)

**הֲלֹא מִצְעָר הִוא** — "Is it not a small thing?"

The city's name **צוֹעַר** (Zo'ar) is explained by the wordplay on **מִצְעָר** (mits'ar) — "a small thing / a trifle." Lot's plea is: let me flee to this small, insignificant city — not the mountain where the danger seems greater (to Lot). The city is spared because of Lot's request — an extraordinary detail. The city that survives the destruction of the plain does so not because of its righteousness but because a righteous man (saved for Abraham's sake) asked for it. The name-etiology embedded in the narrative (**עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שֵׁם-הָעִיר צוֹעַר** — v. 22) is a characteristic feature of Genesis narrative: places receive their names from the events that occur there or the words spoken there.

---

### On 19:24 — וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר... מֵאֵת יְהוָה: The Double YHWH Reference (Key Crux)

**וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר עַל-סְדֹם וְעַל-עֲמֹרָה גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ מֵאֵת יְהוָה מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם** — "And the LORD rained down upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah sulfur and fire — from the LORD — from the heavens."

This verse contains one of the most discussed textual cruxes in the Torah: YHWH appears twice — as the subject of the action (**וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר** — "and the LORD rained") and as the source from which the sulfur and fire come (**מֵאֵת יְהוָה** — "from the LORD"). The same God appears to be both the acting subject and the stated source — "the LORD rained... from the LORD."

**The exegetical options:**

1. **Two distinct divine figures / Two hypostases:** The most literal reading generates the question: are two different YHWHs referenced? This reading was adopted by early Christian interpreters (Justin Martyr, Tertullian, and others in the patristic period) as evidence for a distinction within the Godhead — one YHWH on earth (the one who had remained with Abraham in ch. 18; cf. 18:22) acting on behalf of YHWH in heaven. On this reading, the one who "rained" is the divine presence engaged with the earthly scene, while "from the LORD in heaven" is the transcendent source. Justin Martyr in his Dialogue with Trypho (ch. 56, 129) cites this verse specifically as evidence that a second divine figure — the Logos — acts on behalf of the Father, the heavenly YHWH.

2. **A scribal or stylistic idiom:** Some scholars explain the double YHWH as a formulaic redundancy common to ancient Near Eastern religious-legal language — the agent of divine action is identified by the name of the deity for whom he acts. "The LORD rained, from the LORD" = "the LORD acted as the LORD's agent." The angels execute the destruction in YHWH's name; v. 13 (**יְהוָה שִׁלְּחָנוּ** — "the LORD sent us") confirms that the angels are YHWH's agents. Yet the subject of v. 24 is not the angels but YHWH himself.

3. **The heavenly YHWH/earthly YHWH distinction:** Some ancient interpreters distinguished between YHWH who accompanies Lot and acts on earth and YHWH "in heaven" as the ultimate source of the judgment. The narrative of Gen. 18 shows YHWH remaining with Abraham on earth (18:22, 33) while the two angels go to Sodom. By the time of 19:24, has the earthly YHWH acted? This reading requires more narrative reconstruction than the text supplies but has been influential.

4. **A poetic/emphatic construction:** The phrase **מֵאֵת יְהוָה מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם** is parenthetical — inserted to specify that the fire and sulfur come from heaven, from the divine source above. "The LORD rained — [this coming] from the LORD out of heaven" — the second YHWH reinforces the heavenly origin and divine authority of the catastrophe. The sulfur-and-fire is not a natural geological event but an act of divine judgment coming from above.

**The EMET translates both occurrences literally** — "the LORD rained... from the LORD... from the heavens" — preserving the full textual weight of the double YHWH. The reader deserves to see the crux exactly as it stands in the Leningrad Codex. Any smoothing would falsify the text.

**גָּפְרִית וָאֵשׁ** — "sulfur and fire." **גָּפְרִית** (gafrit) — sulfur, brimstone — a naturally occurring mineral in the Dead Sea region, associated with volcanic or seismic events. The combination of sulfur and fire is the signature destruction of Sodom across the prophetic tradition: Isaiah 34:9 (Edom's judgment uses the same image), Psalm 11:6, Ezekiel 38:22, and in the NT: Revelation 14:10, 19:20, 20:10, 21:8. The image of the burning sulfur-plain has become one of the most enduring images of divine judgment in Western thought.

---

### On 19:26 — נְצִיב מֶלַח: Pillar of Salt (Key Crux)

**וַתְּהִי נְצִיב מֶלַח** — "and she became a pillar of salt."

**נְצִיב** (netsiv) — "pillar, post, statue, standing thing" — from the root **נָצַב** (natsav), "to stand, to station." A **נְצִיב מֶלַח** is literally "a standing thing of salt" — a salt-pillar, a salt-statue. The image is stark and sudden: she looked back and was transformed into a static standing form of salt.

**Why salt?** The Dead Sea region (near Sodom's traditional location) is characterized by extensive salt formations — the southern end of the Dead Sea includes Mount Sodom (Har Sedom), a geological formation of nearly pure halite (rock salt) with salt pillars naturally formed by erosion. In antiquity these formations were visible and striking; the tradition that one of them was Lot's wife appears in Josephus (Antiquities I.11.4: "I have seen it"), in Wisdom of Solomon 10:7 ("a standing pillar of salt, a monument of an unbelieving soul"), and continues into later tradition. The geological reality of the salt formations near the Dead Sea has generated speculation, but the text presents the transformation as a supernatural judgment — the immediate consequence of violating the command of v. 17.

**The theological image:** Salt preserves — but it also arrests, fixes, renders sterile. The pillar of salt is the image of a life frozen in its backward orientation, fixed at the moment of its wrong turning, unable to go forward. It is simultaneously a monument and a warning. The text does not name Lot's wife (she is identified only by her relation to Lot, as in v. 15-16: "his wife"). Her namelessness in the narrative increases the stark quality of her end; she is reduced to a geological marker — a pillar by which travelers pass.

**The LXX (Vaticanus):** **καὶ ἐγένετο στήλη ἁλός** — "and she became a salt pillar." **Στήλη** (stele) — a standing stone, a memorial pillar. The LXX chooses **στήλη** rather than a word for column or post — a stele is specifically a commemorative stone, a monument. The LXX thus emphasizes the memorial/monumental quality: she became not merely a geological feature but a memorial stone — a monument to her own backward-looking.

---

### On 19:27–28 — Abraham Sees the Destruction (The View from Mamre)

**וַיַּשְׁקֵף עַל-פְּנֵי סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה** — "and he looked down over the face of Sodom and Gomorrah."

**וַיַּשְׁקֵף** (wayyashqef) — from **שָׁקַף** (shaqaf), "to look down, to look out, to gaze from a height." The same verb was used in 18:16 when the men "looked down upon the face of Sodom" before departing — the divine gaze preceding judgment. Now Abraham gazes on the result of that judgment. He sees smoke rising like the smoke of a furnace (**כְּקִיטֹר הַכִּבְשָׁן**). The furnace-image echoes the **תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן** (smoking furnace) of 15:17 — the covenant theophany in which YHWH passed through the pieces as a smoking oven. The destruction of Sodom is a fire event that mirrors the covenant-fire; the destruction confirms that the Judge of all the earth does indeed do justice.

Abraham comes back to exactly the place where he stood before the LORD (18:22, 33) — the same spot, now the morning after. He stood and interceded; he returns to see the outcome. The narrative grants Abraham the gaze of a witness — he sees what his intercession did not prevent. There were not even ten righteous in Sodom. The city is gone. Only the smoke remains.

**The note of memory in v. 29:** "God remembered Abraham" — the connection is made explicit. Abraham's intercession was not in vain, even though Sodom burned. It was in vain for Sodom — but not for Lot. The intercessor who could not save the city saved the one family within it.

---

### On 19:29 — וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים: God Remembered Abraham (Key Crux — Divine Memory)

**וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-אַבְרָהָם** — "and God remembered Abraham."

**זָכַר** (zakhar) — "to remember" — in the Hebrew Bible, divine remembering is never merely cognitive recall; it is the activation of covenantal commitment. When God "remembers" in Genesis and Exodus, action follows: "God remembered Noah" (8:1) — and the flood receded. "God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" (Exod. 2:24) — and the rescue from Egypt began. The divine memory is always performative — to remember is to act on behalf of the one remembered.

**אֱלֹהִים** (Elohim) — here the text uses Elohim rather than YHWH. The chapter has used both names: **יְהוָה** (YHWH) in the direct-speech contexts (vv. 13, 14, 16, 24) and **אֱלֹהִים** (Elohim) in the narrative summary of v. 29. This shift is consistent with the source-critical analysis of the Pentateuch (J and E/P traditions) but in the final canonical text functions as a theological variation: the covenantal personal name YHWH governs the dialogic scenes; Elohim governs the reflective summary of providential care.

**מִתּוֹךְ הַהֲפֵכָה** — "from the midst of the overturning." The noun **הֲפֵכָה** (hafekhah) is from **הָפַךְ** (hafakh) — "to overturn, to turn upside down, to overthrow." The same verb appears in v. 25 (**וַיַּהֲפֹךְ** — "and he overturned"), in 19:21, 29, and becomes the standard prophetic term for Sodom's fate: **כְּמַהְפֵּכַת סְדֹם** (Amos 4:11; Isa. 13:19; Jer. 49:18; 50:40). The "overturning of Sodom" is one of the fixed images of divine judgment in the Hebrew prophets — equivalent in force to "the flood of Noah." Lot is rescued from the midst of this overturning.

---

### On 19:30–38 — Lot and His Daughters in the Cave: The Origin of Moab and Ammon (Key Crux)

The final unit of Genesis 19 (vv. 30-38) is one of the most uncomfortable passages in the Hebrew Bible: Lot's daughters, believing themselves to be the last human survivors, intoxicate their father and sleep with him on successive nights, each conceiving a son — Moab and Ben-Ammi, the ancestors of the Moabites and Ammonites.

**The narrative's framing and structure:**

The daughters' reasoning is stated clearly: **אֵין אִישׁ בָּאָרֶץ לָבוֹא עָלֵינוּ כְּדֶרֶךְ כָּל-הָאָרֶץ** — "there is no man in the land to come in to us according to the way of all the earth" (v. 31). They believe the destruction was total — a universal catastrophe like the flood — and that they and their father are the last survivors. This misperception (there are of course other humans alive — they can see Zoar) drives their decision: they act from a sincere, if mistaken, belief that they must preserve humanity through their father's seed. The narrator presents their reasoning without directly endorsing or condemning it — a characteristic feature of the Hebrew narrative style noted throughout Genesis.

**וְלֹא-יָדַע בְּשִׁכְבָהּ וּבְקוּמָהּ** — "and he did not know when she lay down and when she arose" (vv. 33, 35). The verb **יָדַע** again — the same word as in v. 5 (the demand of the men of Sodom), v. 8 (the daughters who had not known a man). The chapter is structured around **yada'** in its multiple senses: the mob's violent demand to "know" the angels; the daughters' untouched state ("have not known a man"); Lot's ignorance of what is done to him while drunk. The verbal echo is surely intentional: the city that demanded violent **yada'** is destroyed; Lot who protected his daughters from the mob's **yada'** is himself subjected to an unknowing **yada'** by those same daughters. The reversal is ironic and pointed.

**The origin-etiology of Moab and Ammon:**

The passage is primarily an origin story — explaining how Israel's two eastern neighbors, the Moabites and Ammonites, came to exist. Both nations are thus descended from Lot, Abraham's nephew, making them distant relatives of Israel — but through an incestuous union. The narrative is Israel's polemical account of Moabite and Ammonite origins: they are Lot's children, they are kin, but they are born of incest and their ancestor emerged from a doomed city. The relationship between Israel and these nations in the subsequent history (hostility with Moab and Ammon in Judges, Numbers, Deuteronomy; friendship in Ruth's story; the Davidic descent through Ruth the Moabitess) is shaped by this origin narrative.

**מוֹאָב** (Moab, v. 37) — the name is explained by a folk etymology: **מֵאָב** — "from father." Moab = "from [my] father." The narrator does not provide a verbal etymology but the meaning is embedded in the narrative context. **בֶּן-עַמִּי** (Ben-Ammi, v. 38) — "son of my people/kin" — likewise an embedded nameplay: the son of Ammon is named "son of my kinsman." Both names encode the nature of their origin: fathered by one's own kin.

**The daughters and moral evaluation:**

The text refuses to moralize. It states what happened; it explains the daughters' reasoning; it records the births and the names. There is no divine rebuke, no punishment, no authorial condemnation. In this it is identical in technique to the story of Judah and Tamar (Gen. 38) — another case of unconventional sexual union in service of preserving seed, presented without editorial judgment. Whether the narrator intends us to see the daughters' act as heroic (they preserved life when they believed none remained), pragmatic, or sinful is left entirely to the reader.

**The structural irony completing Genesis 19:** Lot began the chapter as the hospitable righteous man protecting guests from sexual violence; he ends it as an unknowing participant in incest with his own daughters. This is not a condemnation of Lot by the narrator — it is a portrayal of how profoundly the destruction has damaged the moral fabric of the survivors. The daughters grew up in Sodom. Their solution to the problem of survival is Sodom's logic — use the body as an instrument — applied now to an extreme situation with genuine, if misguided, moral intent.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 19

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 19:1 | שְׁנֵי הַמַּלְאָכִים | οἱ δύο ἄγγελοι | LXX: "the two angels" — exact rendering. **ἄγγελος** (angelos) = messenger/angel. The LXX also uses the definite article, treating them as identified figures. |
| 19:1 | לוֹט יֹשֵׁב בְּשַׁעַר-סְדֹם | Λὼτ δὲ ἐκάθητο παρὰ τὴν πύλην Σοδόμων | LXX: "Lot was sitting beside the gate of Sodom." **πύλη** (pyle) = gate. The LXX adds **δέ** (de, "and/but") creating a contrast particle — "the angels came, and Lot was sitting." |
| 19:2 | לִינוּ | καταλύσατε | LXX: "lodge, stop over, dissolve [the journey]" — **καταλύω** (kataluo) = to dissolve, to stop traveling, to lodge. This verb recurs in NT contexts of lodging (Luke 9:12; 19:7: "he has gone in to lodge"). |
| 19:3 | מִשְׁתֶּה | δοχήν | LXX: "reception, welcome feast" — **δοχή** (doche) from **δέχομαι** (dechomai, "to receive"). The MT's **מִשְׁתֶּה** (mishteh) = feast, drinking-feast; the LXX's **δοχή** emphasizes the welcoming aspect. |
| 19:3 | מַצּוֹת | ἀζύμους | LXX: "unleavened things" — **ἄζυμος** (azymos) = without leaven. Exact rendering. The LXX's word is the source of the English "azymes" (unleavened bread). |
| 19:5 | וְנֵדְעָה אֹתָם | ἵνα συγγενώμεθα αὐτοῖς | LXX: "that we might come together with them" — **συγγίνομαι** (syngínomai) = to come together with, to have intercourse with. The LXX renders **yada'** with an unambiguous sexual term. There is no ambiguity in the Greek: the LXX translators understood the demand as sexual. |
| 19:8 | לֹא-יָדְעוּ אִישׁ | αἳ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν ἄνδρα | LXX: "who have not known a man" — exact rendering, using the same **γινώσκω** (ginosko) as for **yada'** in v. 5. The LXX's consistency confirms the sexual meaning of **yada'** in both verses. |
| 19:11 | בַּסַּנְוֵרִים | ἀορασίᾳ | LXX: "with blindness/inability to see" — **ἀορασία** (aorasia, literally "un-seeing-ness"). A rare Greek word matching the rare Hebrew. |
| 19:16 | בְּחֶמְלַת יְהוָה עָלָיו | ἐν τῷ φείσασθαι κύριον αὐτοῦ | LXX: "in the sparing of the Lord for him / because the Lord spared him" — **φείδομαι** (pheidomai) = to spare, to hold back from harming. The LXX shifts from the MT's **חֶמְלָה** (compassion, tender mercy) to sparing/restraint from harm. The MT's word is more emotionally rich; the LXX's more juridical. |
| 19:17 | הִמָּלֵט עַל-נַפְשֶׁךָ | σῴζε τὴν σεαυτοῦ ψυχήν | LXX: "save your own soul/life" — **σῴζω** (sozo) = to save. The MT's **מָלַט** (malat) = to escape, to slip away, to be delivered; the LXX uses the word for salvation/rescue. **Sozo** in this context carries the full LXX and NT weight of the salvation vocabulary. |
| 19:24 | וַיהוָה הִמְטִיר... מֵאֵת יְהוָה | καὶ κύριος ἔβρεξεν... παρὰ κυρίου | LXX: "and the Lord rained... from the Lord." The double **κύριος** (kyrios) matches the double YHWH of the MT exactly. The LXX preserves the crux. |
| 19:26 | נְצִיב מֶלַח | στήλη ἁλός | LXX: "salt stele/pillar" — **στήλη** (stele) = a standing stone, memorial stone. The LXX emphasizes the monumental/memorial quality: a commemorative stone of salt. |
| 19:29 | וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים | ἐμνήσθη ὁ θεός | LXX: "God remembered" — **μιμνήσκομαι** (mimneskomai) = exact rendering. The LXX uses **θεός** (theos) matching **אֱלֹהִים** (Elohim) — as in the MT. |
| 19:37 | מוֹאָב | Μωαβ | LXX: "Moab" — transliteration. |
| 19:38 | בֶּן-עַמִּי | Αμμαν | LXX: "Amman" — the LXX transliterates the place-name rather than the personal name (**בֶּן-עַמִּי**), anticipating the national name **עַמּוֹן** (Ammon). |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant extant Genesis 19 fragments have been identified from Qumran. The manifest notes confirm: "DSS: No significant Genesis 19 fragments extant." The Samaritan Pentateuch agrees closely with the Leningrad Codex text.

**Josephus (Antiquities I.11.1-4):** Josephus paraphrases the Sodom narrative. He identifies the visitors as angels (**ἄγγελοι**), describes the mob's demand as an attempt to violate the strangers, records Lot's rescue, and notes explicitly (I.11.4) that he himself saw the salt pillar: "But Lot's wife continually turning back to view the city as she went from it, and being too fond of the things she left behind, was changed into a pillar of salt: for I have seen it, and it remains at this day." Josephus's claim of personal sighting is historically improbable (he died c. 100 CE; any actual pillar formation he saw would have been a natural feature of the Dead Sea region interpreted through the tradition) but witnesses to the ancient currency of the tradition.

**Wisdom of Solomon 10:6-8:** "Wisdom rescued a righteous man when the ungodly were perishing; he escaped the fire that descended on the Five Cities... Of whose wickedness even to this day the waste land that smokes is a testimony, and plants bearing fruit that does not ripen, and a standing pillar of salt is a monument of an unbelieving soul." The Wisdom tradition (c. 1st century BCE/CE) regards Lot as a type of the righteous person rescued by Wisdom, and his wife as a monument to unbelief.

**Philo (De Abrahamo 133-146; De Fuga et Inventione 121-122):** Philo allegorizes the Sodom narrative extensively. The destruction of Sodom is the destruction of the passions — the five cities of the plain represent the five senses, which are consumed by divine judgment when they become instruments of vice. Lot's escape is the soul's escape from sense-perception to reason. Lot's wife becoming salt is the soul that turns back toward the sensory world and becomes fixed in its orientation. The daughters represent the fruits of reason born after the destruction of passion — an extraordinary allegorical reversal that transforms the incest narrative into a story of philosophical virtue.

**New Testament connections:**
- Luke 17:28-32 — Jesus cites the days of Lot and explicitly says "remember Lot's wife" as a warning against attachment to earthly things when judgment comes.
- 2 Peter 2:6-8 — Sodom and Gomorrah are cited as examples of divine judgment; Lot is described as "righteous Lot, greatly distressed by the sensual conduct of the wicked" — a notably charitable reading of Lot's character in light of ch. 19's complexities.
- Jude 7 — Sodom and Gomorrah are cited as "undergoing a punishment of eternal fire" as a warning example, with their sin described as pursuing "different flesh" (**σαρκὸς ἑτέρας** — another/strange flesh).
- Revelation 11:8 — Jerusalem is called "the great city that symbolically is called Sodom and Egypt" — the name Sodom becomes a cipher for any city marked by injustice, violence, and the persecution of God's witnesses.

**Targum Onkelos on 19:5:** Renders **וְנֵדְעָה** as **וְנִשְׁמְשִׁינּוֹן** — "that we might use them / have intercourse with them" — an unambiguous sexual rendering, confirming the rabbinic reading of the demand.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on 19:24:** Provides an extended theological comment on the double YHWH: "And the Word of the LORD caused to descend upon Sodom and Gomorrah brimstone and fire from before the LORD from the heavens." The Aramaic **מֵימְרָא** (Memra — Word) of the LORD serves as an intermediary — the agent of the destruction is the divine Word, acting on behalf of the transcendent LORD in heaven. This Targumic reading anticipates the hypostatic interpretation and connects to the broader Targumic theology of the Memra.
